exemplar.title: How does changing the volume of water in a water bottle rocket impact the maximum altitude it can achieve?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does changing the volume of water in a water bottle rocket impact the maximum altitude it can achieve?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear and effective communication of topic with sustained focus on maximum altitude throughout the report
Precisely stated, narrow and measurable research question strongly linked to every section
Thorough, wellâstructured procedural steps and clear identification of variables
weaknesses: Occasional minor digressions (anecdotal material) dilute academic precision
Justification for key methodological choices (e.g., trial number, safety considerations) is brief or absent
Minimal discussion of measurement uncertainties for fin dimensions and flowârate assumptions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates a high level of clarity and focus: the phenomenon is described accurately, the research question is explicit and every section links back to it. Methodological coverage is mostly complete, with only minor gaps in justification and safety/uncertainty discussion. On balance, this performance sits solidly in the 5â6 band but falls just short of perfect precision, meriting 5/6.

strengths: Correct application of Newtonâs laws and kinematic principles with relevant numerical estimates
Mostly accurate use of key physics terminology and consistent definition of symbols
Appropriate handling of raw data uncertainties and unit conversions in tables
weaknesses: Simplifications (constant flowârate, drag coefficient) and occasional dimensional slips limit coherence
Inconsistent symbol notation (e.g. drag coefficient âC_pâ vs. âC_Dâ) and minor formatting errors
Symbolic notation (vector arrows) and source justifications lack full clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows good subject knowledge and generally accurate use of terminology. However, recurring simplifications, notation inconsistencies and a few dimensional/formatting errors prevent it from reaching the top band. Overall performance aligns with the lower half of the 3â4 band, so 3/6 is appropriate.

strengths: Systematic data analysis with averaging, uncertainty propagation, and clear linkage to the research question
Logical argument development from background through modeling to conclusions
Thoughtful identification of key error sources (wind, tracking) with practical suggestions for improvement
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on untested assumptions (humidity, constant drag) without quantitative justification
Limited sophistication in evaluating source reliability and statistical robustness
Trendline modeling based on few data points and without confidence intervals
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis and argument are wellâstructured and clearly relevant to the research question, placing the work in the 4â6 band for Critical Thinking. However, assumptions remain unsupported, and critical evaluation lacks quantitative depth. This performance merits 4/12, reflecting solid but not fully developed critical thinking.

strengths: Overall structure follows Extended Essay conventions (title page, pagination, sections, figures and tables)
Effective embedding and captioning of figures and tables to support comprehension
Clear layout that generally aids readability
weaknesses: Table of Contents lacks page numbers, hindering navigation
Lowâresolution graphics and overlapping text in places reduce clarity
Inconsistent heading styles and occasional missing units in labels
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation adheres well to formal requirements with only minor lapses in navigational aids and image quality. These issues prevent a flawless layout but sit firmly in the 3â4 band, warranting 3/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key physics terms (drag coefficient, mass flow rate, thrust) are used correctly and symbols are defined.  Occasional misspellings ("charecteristics", âexperincedâ) and inconsistent subscripts/units keep usage from being âconsistently accurate,â but accuracy is mostly maintained, placing the work comfortably in the 3-4 band.

The argument progresses logically: background â variables â data â model â evaluation.  Reasoning supports the conclusion that ~300 ml gives highest altitude.  Some leaps ("rocket with 500 ml produces most thrust") and limited linking of theory to every data point prevent the sophistication expected in the top bands, so a strong 5 within 4-6 is appropriate.

Method covers materials, step-by-step procedure, repetition, control of key variables and treatment of uncertainties.  However, justification of choices (e.g., why 3 bar, why five trials) is brief and the treatment of safety/ethical issues is absent.  Source selection for coefficients is limited to websites/Wikipedia.  Range is therefore âmostly completeâ rather than fully comprehensive â top of the 3-4 band.

The student applies Newtonâs laws, thrust, drag and kinematics in a largely correct manner and incorporates numerical estimates.  Some simplifications (constant flow rate, constant drag coefficient) and a few dimensional slips limit coherence, but overall understanding is clear and sources are generally relevant.  Quality matches the middle band (3-4) with sufficient but not outstanding mastery.

The student comments on reliability, identifies wind and human tracking as error sources, proposes extra cameras/repetition and briefly evaluates sources.  Reflection is present but mainly qualitative; there is little quantitative error analysis beyond % discrepancy with a simulator.  This meets âsome critical analysisâ but is not sustained throughout, aligning with the lower end of 4-6 (score 4).

The student communicates the study of water-bottle rockets clearly, states the purpose up-front and keeps references to maximum altitude throughout the text. Although spelling/grammar errors are frequent, they do not obscure meaning and the focus on the chosen phenomenon is maintained from introduction to conclusion.  Minor digressions (childhood anecdote, speculation about future questions) prevent âexcellentâ precision, so a solid but not perfect performance in the 5-6 band is most appropriate.

Figures are embedded, captioned and referenced; tables are readable.  Some images are low-resolution and text overlaps in a few places, but overall the layout aids comprehension.  This fulfils criteria for effective presentation, meriting 3 points in the top band.

The research question â âHow does changing the volume of water in a water bottle rocket impact the maximum altitude it can achieve?â â is explicitly stated, narrow and measurable.  Every section (variables, data tables, analysis) links back to this single dependent variable, showing a strong connection between question and discussion.  Focus wavers slightly when the essay generalises to âlarger rockets,â hence a 5 rather than 6.

Data are gathered systematically, averaged and propagated; graphs show trendlines; a basic quantitative model is compared with a simulator.  Although assumptions are heavy and analysis is limited to one polynomial fit, it is clearly linked to the RQ and goes beyond mere description.  This represents good, but not excellent, analytical treatment â mid-band (â5/6).

The essay follows EE conventions: title page, contents, labelled sections, tables, figures and citation list.  Pagination exists and word count is stated.  Minor issues (TOC page numbers missing, inconsistent heading styles) stop it being flawless, hence a 3 within the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The net force equation $F\uparrow = F_T - F_G - F_D$ is well presented, linking theoretical framework to the model.
The lengthy anecdotal background dilutes the academic focus. Condense personal narrative to maintain precision in topic communication.
Ensure consistent capitalization of the pronoun âIâ in the introduction to uphold formal academic tone.
The research question is articulated clearly and precisely, establishing a narrow, measurable focus that frames the entire investigation effectively.
The word count is stated accurately (3992 words) and remains within the 4000-word limit, satisfying the formatting requirement.
Consider refining the title to reflect the specific focus on water volume optimization, for example âOptimizing Water Volume in Bottle Rockets for Maximum Altitude.â
The Table of Contents lacks corresponding page numbers, making navigation difficult. Include accurate page references for each section.
Heading style in the contents uses inconsistent capitalization (âTable Of Contents:â). Align with MLA/APA conventions for uniform formatting.
The introduction concludes by restating the research question, reinforcing focus and linking personal interest to the investigationâs purpose.
The resolution of the FBD image is low, causing overlap and potential misinterpretation. Use higher-res graphics.
Explicitly reference axes and vector directions in the diagram text to reinforce conceptual clarity.
Variables section identifies independent, dependent, and controls clearly, showing a structured methodological approach.
The description of Newtonâs third law is accurate and clearly applies to rocket thrust, demonstrating solid subject knowledge.
The drag coefficient is labeled âC_pâ in text but appears as $C_D$ in equations. Standardize symbol notation for clarity.
Provide a citation for the chosen drag coefficient beyond the general web reference [1], ideally from a peer-reviewed source.
Area calculations correctly convert units and combine bottle and fin areas, demonstrating strong application of geometric concepts.
The choice of fin dimensions lacks measurement uncertainty; include Â± values for fin width and length in the methodology.
Incorporate uncertainty propagation for the measured radius when computing cross-sectional area to strengthen critical evaluation.
Figure 1 is well captioned and sourced, aiding reader understanding of the free-body diagrams.
Ensure uniform citation style (e.g., APA or MLA) across all entries, particularly for online sources with missing publication details.
The assumption of constant flow rate and exit velocity is stated but not justified; discuss potential variation during powered flight.
Consider measuring and reporting air temperature as a controlled variable to evaluate its influence on air density and drag.
The camera-mount controlled variable description continues beyond the line, creating a readability issue.
Correct misplaced overline placeholders ($\overline{\phantom{0}}$) in the controlled variables list for clear mathematical formatting.
Assuming humidity effects are negligible is unsupported; estimate their impact on air density to justify this assumption.
Quantify the humidity assumption by calculating potential percentage change in air density at launch conditions.
The procedural steps are thorough and sequential, demonstrating a well-structured methodology.
Safety considerations (e.g., eye protection, stable launch area) are omitted. Include a brief ethics/safety section.
Justify the choice of five trials per volume with reference to statistical confidence and error reduction.
Table 1.1 includes uncertainties and clear labels for raw data, demonstrating correct handling of measurements.
The âTime to Apogeeâ header omits its unit (s). Include units in every column heading for full clarity.
Consider embedding a small summary chart of all five runs in the main text rather than relegating full raw data to an appendix.
Processed data in Table 1.2 correctly applies averaging and uncertainty propagation, reflecting coherent data handling.
The method for range computation (half-range) appears in text but isnât explicitly defined as absolute or percentage; clarify in caption.
A simple bar chart of average altitude vs. volume would enhance visual interpretation of processed data.
Symbol $F\uparrow$ is introduced but the arrowâs significance isnât fully explained; clarify vector notation in the text.
Include unit âNâ after each term in the net force equation display to reinforce dimensional correctness.
Mass flow rate calculation identifies 5âkg/s but omits uncertainty; discuss timing measurement error in the video analysis.
Explain any discrepancy between assumed nozzle radius and the one measured in practice to bolster methodological transparency.
Net force result (124.68âN) is clearly stated, showing coherent link between theoretical model and experimental values.
The statement that 500âml produces most thrust contradicts earlier experimental data showing 300âml as optimal; reconcile this discrepancy.
Discuss how the polynomial fit trendline informs why 300âml yields higher altitude than the net thrust model alone suggests.
Trendline is derived from only three ratio points (6â%,13â%,20â%); extend to include 26â% and 33â% for comprehensive modelling.
Include confidence intervals or standard error estimates for the trendline coefficients to convey statistical robustness.
Figure 5 effectively presents the ratio vs. altitude relationship, supporting the modelâs generalization visually.
The axes in Figure 5 lack labels and units, reducing clarity. Add axis titles (âRatio of Water (%)â, âMax Altitude (m)â).
Increase font size of axis labels and trendline equation in Figure 5 to enhance readability in print.
Evaluation acknowledges human-tracking error and suggests solutions, demonstrating thoughtful critical reflection.
Systematic errors in video calibration (lens distortion, parallax) are not discussed; include these to deepen evaluation.
Recommend using an anemometer during launches to quantify wind speed and assess its effect on altitude variability.
The source evaluation notes reliability but lacks critique of individual source limitations (e.g., Wikipedia).
Discuss the potential bias and variable accuracy of crowd-sourced platforms like Wikipedia when selected as references.
References in the Works Cited list lack hanging indent formatting; apply consistent indentation for readability.
